summary: DeVault excoriates the Python ecosystem for its chaotic, ever-proliferating packaging landscape and its disregard for Linux distribution maintainers. He argues that the constant reinvention of packaging tools—distutils, setuptools, pip, poetry, conda, and many more—has created an unmanageable mess. As an Alpine Linux package maintainer, he finds that none of these tools address distro needs, breaking backwards compatibility while ignoring repeated pleas from every major distribution. He calls on the PSF to exercise strong leadership, pick one solution, and commit to stability for the next 30 years.
key_points: Python's packaging ecosystem is a disaster of competing, incompatible tools that keeps getting worse rather than better
Linux distribution maintainers are systematically ignored despite being critical stakeholders in Python's ecosystem for 30 years
Modern Python packaging tools are designed for a reckless Docker-and-virtualenv world that treats security and stability as afterthoughts
The PSF needs to exercise decisive leadership, pick one packaging solution, and commit to long-term stability even if it means hurting feelings
PEP-517 and PEP-518 are insufficient because they tolerate the proliferation of build systems rather than consolidating around one
money_quotes: The Python community is obsessed with reinventing the wheel, over and over and over and over and over and over again.

Every one of these package managers is designed for a reckless world in which programmers chuck packages wholesale into ~/.pip, set up virtualenvs and pin their dependencies to 10 versions and 6 vulnerabilities ago, and ship their computers directly into production in Docker containers which aim to do the minimum amount necessary to make their user's private data as insecure as possible.
I manage my Python packages in the only way which I think is sane: installing them from my Linux distribution's package manager.

Do you just feel that it's time to shrug off the "legacy" systems we represent and embrace the brave new world of serverless cloud-scale regulation-arbitrage move-fast-and-break-things culture of the techbro startup?
Right now, upstream neglect is destroying the Python ecosystem. The situation is grave, and we need strong upstream leadership right now.
Python ought to stop trying to avoid hurting anyone's feelings and pick one.
I simply can't handle it anymore, and I have been hard at work removing Python from my stack.
key_insight: Python's refusal to exercise decisive leadership over its packaging ecosystem is alienating Linux distributions—the very communities that have sustained Python for decades.
